topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped book. Subject line: "Update regarding Sarah's surprise." My fingers hovered over the screen, then stabbed at the 'Open' button with unnecessary force. The words swam before my eyes, a cascade of disappointing news. *Cancelled.* A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards like spilled ink. I reread the message, the carefully constructed cheerfulness of the original invitation mocking me. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, my jaw tight. I started running a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically undo the disappointment. Why? Just why? I muttered the question under my breath, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone screen, the bright light reflecting off my suddenly clammy palms. It was 10 PM. The silence in the room felt thick, suffocating. The confirmation had been vague, then definitive: the party was off. I let out a slow, shaky breath that tasted like ash. I picked up the remote and clicked through the channels with mechanical indifference, unable to focus on anything. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy weight settling on my spine. "Are you serious?" I finally managed to say, the words sounding hollow even to my own ears. I dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion as if it had burned me, and just sat there, letting the sheer absurdity of the situation wash over me in a wave of weary resignation.

[story 3]

When I saw the notification pop up—the official communication confirming the event was scrubbed from the calendar—I felt a sudden, sharp prickle behind my eyes. I blinked rapidly, trying to clear the sudden blur. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. I walked over to the window, looking out at the street as if the answer to this puzzle could somehow be written in the clouds. A low growl rumbled in my chest, a sound born of sheer, unadulterated frustration. I turned back to the computer, my movements jerky and impatient as I scrolled through the cancellation details, each line a fresh offense against the planned joy. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing in the suddenly too-quiet room.
